payload.instructionText: Finish with sugar-free cinnamon syrup to taste.

Caramelize the banana: slice and cook in a nonstick pan over medium heat with a sprinkle of sweetener until golden, ~2-3 min per side. Add the pumpkin to the same pan and cook 3-5 min, stirring, until thickened and nutty. In the last ~45-60 sec, stir the ground cinnamon into the hot fruit to toast it — it should turn fragrant and deepen a shade. Toast it in the fruit, not in a dry pan: loose ground cinnamon scorches in seconds, but the fruit's moisture buffers it so it blooms evenly. Remove and COOL COMPLETELY (hot mix will scramble the eggs).

Beat the light cream cheese alone until smooth, then add yogurt, blended cottage cheese, protein powder, sweetener, vanilla, lemon, and vanilla pudding mix. Sift/whisk in the AP flour smooth (no lumps) and mix just until combined — don't overwork it. Warm the bowl ~15 sec if it clumps.

Pour into a parchment-lined 9-inch pan. Tap on the counter to level and pop bubbles. For the topping, toast the graham crumbs with the garnish cinnamon in a dry pan over medium heat ~1 min, stirring, until fragrant, then let cool. Scatter over the cheesecake after baking so they stay crisp.

payload.text: The cooled fruit is non-negotiable — even slightly warm banana/pumpkin will begin cooking the eggs the moment they meet in s4, giving you scrambled curds in the batter.

Blooming the 2 tsp cinnamon in the hot fruit (not a dry pan) is what the step says, and it's right: the moisture prevents scorching while the heat opens the aromatics — 45-60 sec is plenty, pull when it deepens a shade.
Don't overmix once the eggs go in — beating air into a lean cheesecake batter causes it to puff, then crack and sink. Low speed, just until the yolks disappear.

45 min is the ceiling, not the target. With cottage cheese + Greek yogurt this lean, pulling at 155-160F center (slight wobble, not wet jiggle) and letting carryover finish is what keeps slices creamy; a fully dry center = rubbery texture.
Scatter the toasted graham crumbs only after baking, just before serving — they absorb moisture from the filling within an hour in the fridge and go soft if added beforehand.
